card.title: Giving Learner Home's navigation the structure it never had.
card.subtitle: Simplified Learner Home's navigation to reduce friction and align the experience across platforms.

sections.title: Giving Learner Home's navigation the structure it never had.
sections.subtitle: Kaplan is a global educational services company with B2C and B2B products. I simplified the navigation to reduce friction and align cross-platform experiences.

meta.team: Product Designer · UX Researcher · Product Manager · Lead Engineer

sections.label: THE PROBLEM
sections.title: Cluttered menus and unclear hierarchies.
sections.body: Learner Home had evolved without a clear navigation logic, leaving users struggling to find what they needed. I redesigned it to reduce friction: fewer clicks, less hunting, and a consistent experience across Kaplan's connected products.

cards.title: The dashboard was quietly doing the nav's job.
cards.body: Users skipped the menu entirely and relied on the quick-link cards to get where they needed to go. The nav had made itself irrelevant.

image.alt: Dashboard quick-link cards before the redesign

cards.title: The side nav and the avatar menu shared key sections.
cards.body: Finding courses, certificates, or account settings meant guessing which menu held them. Browse Catalog added unnecessary hierarchy.

image.alt: Side nav and avatar menu overlap

cards.title: On desktop, the side nav was one hidden tap away.
cards.body: Primary navigation lived behind a hamburger menu on desktop. Most users didn't open it, even though it held the actions they needed most.

image.alt: Desktop hamburger menu hiding primary navigation

sections.label: A/B TESTING
sections.title: One option for cross-platform consistency, one option for cleaner hierarchy.
sections.body: First, I simplified the hierarchy for a cleaner structure. However, it created a larger gap with Atom, where users spend most of their time, and its team had other priorities than a navigation refactor. Before committing, I ran an A/B test to see if it was worth it.

sections.label: THE APPROACH
sections.title: The difference between what users say and what users do.
sections.body: I went with Design 2, giving each menu a clear role: student info in the avatar, navigation in the side nav, with a handful of labels updated to match learner language. 78% said they preferred Design 1, but Design 2 performed better on task completion:

sections.title: Can a treasure hunt fix a broken loyalty loop?
